questions: Locate the 'UNIX / Linux Tutorial for Beginners' authored by M. Stonebank (University of Surrey) around October 2001, which is structured into exactly eight tutorials ranging from file management to 'UNIX variables'. Identify the specific Creative Commons license applied to this material. Based on the license deed associated with this tutorial, list the two primary 'Freedoms' (what you are free to do) and the three specific 'Terms' (conditions) that must be followed.

Locate the 'UNIX / Linux Tutorial for Beginners' originally created by M. Stonebank at the University of Surrey (dated October 2001). Determine the specific Creative Commons license configuration (attributes such as NonCommercial, ShareAlike, etc.) under which this educational resource is distributed.

Once identified, locate the '4.0 International' version of this specific license configuration. Compare the terms of this license against the standard Creative Commons Attribution 4.0 International (CC BY 4.0) license. Specifically, detail the differences regarding:
1. **Commercial Rights**: What does each license allow or restrict?
2. **Adaptation/Remixing**: What are the obligations for sharing adaptations (ShareAlike) for each?

Provide the Canonical URL for the 4.0 version of the tutorial's license configuration as part of your answer.

rounds.question: Locate the 'UNIX / Linux Tutorial for Beginners' authored by M. Stonebank (University of Surrey) around October 2001, which is structured into exactly eight tutorials ranging from file management to 'UNIX variables'. Identify the specific Creative Commons license applied to this material. Based on the license deed associated with this tutorial, list the two primary 'Freedoms' (what you are free to do) and the three specific 'Terms' (conditions) that must be followed.
checklist_width: Freedom 1: Share (copy and redistribute the material in any medium or format)
Freedom 2: Adapt (remix, transform, and build upon the material)
Term 1: Attribution (give appropriate credit, provide a link, indicate changes)
Term 2: NonCommercial (may not use the material for commercial purposes)
Term 3: ShareAlike (must distribute contributions under the same license as the original)
checklist_depth: Target Entity: UNIX / Linux Tutorial for Beginners by M. Stonebank
License Identification: Creative Commons Attribution-NonCommercial-ShareAlike (specifically version 2.0 based on the hidden source URL, though 'BY-NC-SA' is the core logic)
rounds.rationale: The question requires deep reasoning to anchor a specific legacy tutorial by author and structure (Stonebank, 8 tutorials). It then requires wide aggregation to follow the specific license link (external to the main text) and retrieve the detailed permissions (freedoms) and conditions (terms) from the license deed.
rounds.meta_context_snippet: [Wide Logic - Target 0]: Notice This is an older version of this license. Compared to previous versions, the 4.0 versions of all CC licenses are more user-friendly and more internationally robust . If
rounds.answer_a:
The "UNIX / Linux Tutorial for Beginners" by M. Stonebank at the University of Surrey is licensed under the **Creative Commons Attribution-NonCommercial-ShareAlike 2.0 UK: England & Wales (CC BY-NC-SA 2.0 UK)** license [1]. According to the legal code and deed for this license, the two primary **Freedoms** granted to users are the right to **Share**—copy and redistribute the material in any medium or format—and the right to **Adapt**—remix, transform, and build upon the material [2].

To exercise these freedoms, users must adhere to three specific **Terms**. First, **Attribution** requires appropriate credit to be given, providing a link to the license and indicating if changes were made. Second, **NonCommercial** mandates that the material may not be used for commercial purposes. Third, **ShareAlike** stipulates that if the user remixes, transforms, or builds upon the material, they must distribute their contributions under the same license as the original [2].

## References
[1] University of Surrey, "UNIX / Linux Tutorial for Beginners," *M. Stonebank*, 2001. https://www.ee.surrey.ac.uk/Teaching/Unix/
[2] Creative Commons, "Attribution-NonCommercial-ShareAlike 2.0 UK: England & Wales," *Creative Commons*. https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc-sa/2.0/uk/
rounds.answer_b:
The "UNIX / Linux Tutorial for Beginners" by M. Stonebank of the University of Surrey, dating from October 2001, is an eight-part introductory guide covering topics from basic file management to Unix variables [1]. The complete tutorial series is licensed under the Creative Commons Attribution-Non-Commercial-Share Alike 2.0 UK: England & Wales Licence [2].

The two primary Freedoms granted by this license deed are: **1)** You are free to copy, distribute and transmit the work, and **2)** You are free to adapt the work (remix, transform, and build upon it) [3].

The three specific Terms that must be followed are: **1)** Attribution — You must attribute the work to M. Stonebank/University of Surrey and include a link to the license; **2)** Non-Commercial — You may not use this work for commercial purposes; and **3)** Share Alike — If you alter, transform, or build upon this work, you must distribute the resulting work only under the same or similar license [4].

## References
[1] Stonebank, M. (2001). *UNIX / Linux Tutorial for Beginners*. University of Surrey. Retrieved from https://www.ee.surrey.ac.uk/Teaching/Unix/
[2] "UNIX / Linux Tutorial for Beginners - Creative Commons Licence." University of Surrey. Retrieved from https://www.ee.surrey.ac.uk/Teaching/Unix/unixtutorial.html
[3] Creative Commons. (n.d.). *Attribution-NonCommercial-ShareAlike 2.0 UK: England & Wales*. Retrieved from https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc-sa/2.0/uk/
[4] Ibid.
rounds.judge_reasoning: Both agents correctly identified the specific license and the associated freedoms/terms. Agent B is rated Better because it explicitly confirmed the structural details of the entity requested in the prompt (mentioning the 'eight-part' structure and specific topics). This provides the user with verification that the agent found the *exact* tutorial requested, rather than a generic one. Agent A provided the correct license info but ignored the context verification. Agent B also provided more granular citations.

lines: -> WINNER is B. Targeting Loser Failure: NONE.

rounds.question:
Locate the 'UNIX / Linux Tutorial for Beginners' originally created by M. Stonebank at the University of Surrey (dated October 2001). Determine the specific Creative Commons license configuration (attributes such as NonCommercial, ShareAlike, etc.) under which this educational resource is distributed.

Once identified, locate the '4.0 International' version of this specific license configuration. Compare the terms of this license against the standard Creative Commons Attribution 4.0 International (CC BY 4.0) license. Specifically, detail the differences regarding:
1. **Commercial Rights**: What does each license allow or restrict?
2. **Adaptation/Remixing**: What are the obligations for sharing adaptations (ShareAlike) for each?

Provide the Canonical URL for the 4.0 version of the tutorial's license configuration as part of your answer.
checklist_width: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 Commercial Rights: Explicitly prohibits use for commercial purposes.
CC BY 4.0 Commercial Rights: Explicitly allows use for any purpose, even commercially.
CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 Adaptation: Requires 'ShareAlike' (contributions must be distributed under the same license).
CC BY 4.0 Adaptation: No 'ShareAlike' requirement (only Attribution is required).
Canonical URL for the tutorial's updated license: https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc-sa/4.0/
checklist_depth: Identified Tutorial: M. Stonebank's UNIX Tutorial (University of Surrey).
Logic Validation: The agent must identify the tutorial's specific license restrictions (NC-SA) to select the correct 4.0 target (BY-NC-SA 4.0) for comparison against the generic BY 4.0.
rounds.rationale: The question uses Deep logic by requiring the agent to identify a specific 2001 legacy tutorial to determine its licensing conditions (NC-SA). It uses Wide aggregation by forcing a comparison between the text of two distinct license deeds (Target 0: CC BY 4.0 and Target 2: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0) to explain specific legal differences in commercial use and redistribution.
rounds.meta_context_snippet: [Wide Logic - Target 0]: Canonical URL https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/4.0/ See the legal code You are free to: Share — copy and redistribute the material in any medium or format for any purpo
rounds.answer_a:
The"UNIX / Linux Tutorial for Beginners" by M. Stonebank is distributed under the **Attribution-NonCommercial-ShareAlike (BY-NC-SA)** license configuration [1]. The 4.0 International version of this specific configuration is formally titled the **Creative Commons Attribution-NonCommercial-ShareAlike 4.0 International (CC BY-NC-SA 4.0)**.

Comparing this license against the standard Creative Commons Attribution 4.0 International (CC BY 4.0) highlights the following functional differences regarding usage rights and derivative works:

1.  **Commercial Rights**: The standard CC BY 4.0 license is highly permissive, explicitly granting users the right to exercise the licensed rights for commercial purposes, including for-profit distribution and monetization. Conversely, the tutorial's CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 license imposes a strict **NonCommercial** restriction. This term forbids using the material for any purpose that is primarily directed at commercial advantage or private monetary compensation. While the CC BY 4.0 allows revenue generation, the tutorial's license limits use to non-commercial educational settings or personal environments [2][3].

2.  **Adaptation/Remixing**: Under the standard CC BY 4.0, users are free to adapt the material and may distribute the resulting adaptation under different license terms, provided the original author is attributed. This allows for adaptations to be relicensed under more restrictive or even proprietary terms. However, the tutorial's license enforces a **ShareAlike** condition. This mandates that if you remix, transform, or build upon the material, you must distribute your contributions under the **same license terms** (CC BY-NC-SA 4.0). This creates a "copyleft" effect, ensuring that the non-commercial prohibition and the share-alike requirement propagate to all subsequent users of the derivative work [2][3].

Canonical URL: https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc-sa/4.0/

## References
[1] University of Surrey, "UNIX / Linux Tutorial for Beginners," *M. Stonebank*, 2001. https://www.ee.surrey.ac.uk/Teaching/Unix/
[2] Creative Commons, "Attribution-NonCommercial-ShareAlike 4.0 International," *Creative Commons*. https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc-sa/4.0/
[3] Creative Commons, "Attribution 4.0 International," *Creative Commons*. https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/4.0/
rounds.answer_b:
The UNIX/Linux Tutorial for Beginners by M. Stonebank at the University of Surrey (October 2001) is distributed under a Creative Commons Attribution-NonCommercial-ShareAlike 2.0 UK: England & Wales license [1]. The 4.0 International version of this specific configuration is CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 International, with canonical URL: https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc-sa/4.0/ [2].

**Commercial Rights Comparison:**
CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 strictly prohibits commercial use through its NonCommercial provision, which forbids exercising any licensed rights in ways "primarily intended for or directed toward commercial advantage or monetary compensation" [3]. This restriction applies universally to the original work and all derivatives, preventing use in paid products, corporate training, advertising, or any revenue-generating context without additional permission. CC BY 4.0 explicitly permits commercial exploitation without limitation, allowing the work and any adaptations to be sold, incorporated into proprietary commercial software, or used in for-profit ventures freely [4].

**Adaptation/Remixing Obligations:**
The ShareAlike clause in CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 creates strong copyleft obligations: all adaptations and derivatives must be licensed under identical terms (CC BY-NC-SA 4.0) or a Creative Commons-compatible license with equivalent elements [3]. This requirement propagates through all downstream modifications, ensuring they remain non-commercial and share-alike. CC BY 4.0 imposes no ShareAlike condition; adaptations can be released under any license—including proprietary or commercial terms—provided the adapter gives proper attribution to the original creator as specified in the license [4]. This fundamental difference means CC BY 4.0 enables proprietary derivative works while CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 requires them to remain open.

## References
[1] Stonebank, M. (2001). *UNIX / Linux Tutorial for Beginners*. University of Surrey. https://www.ee.surrey.ac.uk/Teaching/Unix/
[2] Creative Commons. (n.d.). *Attribution-NonCommercial-ShareAlike 4.0 International*. https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc-sa/4.0/
[3] Creative Commons. (n.d.). *License Deed: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0*. https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc-sa/4.0/legalcode
[4] Creative Commons. (n.d.). *License Deed: CC BY 4.0*. https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/4.0/legalcode
rounds.judge_reasoning: Both agents correctly identified the license attributes (BY-NC-SA) and provided accurate comparisons against CC BY 4.0. However, Agent B is the winner because it demonstrated superior retrieval precision by identifying the specific legacy license version used on the original tutorial site (CC BY-NC-SA 2.0 UK: England & Wales), whereas Agent A only provided the general configuration. Agent B's inclusion of this specific detail proves it accessed the ground truth source more effectively. Both agents followed formatting constraints well.
